did.physdesc: Correspondence, photographs, negatives, audio recordings, newsclippings, published material, scrapbooks, memorabilia.
did.abstract: Material relating to the life and work of Liberty Hyde Bailey.

eventgrp.event: Liberty Hyde Bailey, Jr. born near South Haven, Michigan
Sarah Harrison Bailey, Liberty Hyde Bailey, Jr.'s mother, dies
Liberty Hyde Bailey, Sr. marries Maria Bridges
Liberty Hyde Bailey, Jr. delivers first public speech, "Birds," to the South Haven Pomological Society and is elected Ornithologist of the organization
Attends Michigan Agricultural College in Lansing, Michigan; studies Botany under the instruction of Dr. William Beal; meets Annette Smith
Graduates from Michigan Agricultural College with a Bachelor of Science degree
Works in Springfield, Illinois as a reporter for the Morning Monitor
Moves to Cambridge, Massachusetts to work as an assistant to Harvard botanist Asa Gray
Marries Annette Smith
Accepts professorship at Michigan Agricultural College; publishes first book "Talks Afield: About Plants and the Science of Plants"
Receives Master of Science degree from Michigan Agricultural College
Daughter Sara May Bailey born
Invited to give a series of lectures at Cornell
Begins work as a Professor of Practical and Experimental Horticulture at Cornell
Daughter Ethel Zoe Bailey born
Appoints Anna Botsford Comstock as first female professor at Cornell
Becomes Dean of New York State College of Agriculture at Cornell
Leads a Commission on Country Life at the request of President Theodore Roosevelt
Appoints Martha Van Rensselaer and Flora Rose as Professors in Home Economics
Retires from position of Dean; begins a Herbarium at home on Sage Place
Travels around the world on plant collecting expeditions
Elected President of the Botanical Society of America
Gives Hortorium to Cornell
Daughter Sara dies
Wife Annette dies
Bailey's idea for a campus arboretum, botanical garden, and research field is realized with the opening of the Cornell Plantations
Bailey misses 90th birthday party in Ithaca because he is on plant collecting trip in West Indies
Bailey dies at his home in Ithaca
United States Post Office issues a commemoration stamp honoring gardening and horticulture in America

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: Personal, family, and professional papers relating to the life and work of Liberty Hyde Bailey and including correspondence, manuscripts, scrapbooks, journals, photographs, publications, genealogical and biographical information. Also records of the Bailey Hortorium. Correspondence and records of the Commission on Country Life. Also includes glass negatives of Bailiwick orchards and house construction; correspondence and agreements with Macmillan Company; photographs; Bailey's correspondence with his brother Marcus and daughter Ethel; and deeds, abstracts and title searches for the Bailey house on Sage Place; war ration books; agreement of gift of the Bailey Hortorium. Includes Bailey correspondence with Macmillan Company. Also, tape recordings (12 cassettes) and transcripts of an interview with Bailey by George H. M. Lawrence, 1951-1952, audio reels with interview by Edith Fox and Jackson Towne, 11/06/1951 and his typewriter and camera.
Includes a 1901 Junior Naturalist Club certificate signed by Bailey.

CD (MS Power Point) with posters prepared at Michigan State University using Cornell photographs, celebrating the 150th anniversary of the birth of Liberty Hyde Bailey, 2008. The posters were exhibited at the 2008 annual meetings of the American Society for Horticultural Science (July) and at the Great Lakes Expo (Michigan Horticultural Society and Michigan Vegetable Growers (December).
Printing block for the Loganberry illustration from "Gentes Herbarum," with page from the journal showing the illustration and a modern print; other various printing blocks, as well.

relatedmaterial.head: RELATED MATERIAL
relatedmaterial.p: See also Liberty Hyde Bailey, New York State College of Agriculture and Agricultural Experiment Station Records, #21-2-84, Liberty Hyde Bailey Forestry Papers, #21-2-576 and College of Agriculture "Dean's Hat" items, #21-2-3144.

p.extref: Liberty Hyde Bailey: A Man for All Seasons exhibition

emph.ref: Series III. College of Agriculture, Cornell, and Education, 1891 - 1950
Series VI. Hortorium and Plant Collecting, 1887 - 1959
Series X. Cyanotype albums and negatives

separatedmaterial.head: SEPARATED MATERIAL
separatedmaterial.p: Several items relating to Bailey including the Bailey plow, portrait painted by Henry S. Hubbell for Bailey Hall, gold medals, canes, are located in the College of Agriculture Dean's Office, Chair of Plant Science's Office, and Mann Library. The Bailey Hortorium also houses Bailey books, publications, photographs, and artwork.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Liberty Hyde Bailey Papers, #21-2-3342. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Album pages no longer remain bound with covers. Some cyanotypes removed from album pages are described separately. Copies of the information on those removed pages are included in the same locations within the albums.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Prints numbered 3.1-3.232. Include squash, apple, grapes, Will Wescott, Prof. Roberts, Prof. HHWing, Prof. HS Williams, Prof. James Law, Prof. JH Comstock, Wm Caldwell, Miss Smith, Mrs. Bailey, strawberry leaves, eggplants, tomatos, plums, Electric light house (green house), flowers, apples, Prof. Prentis, LH Bailey, leaves, beans, blackberries

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Prints numbered 1-88. Include plums, plum trees, old homestead South Haven Mich, lillies, radishes, dewberries, peach trees in Michigan, begonias, electric light experiment, horseradish, flowers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Prints numbered 89-184. Include parsley, cauliflower, strawberry plants, apples, pears, plums, cherries, chestnuts, mushroom spawn, potato plants, tomato plants, Maxwells plum and apple orchards, Willards vineyards, gooseberries, weeping beech, weeping birch, weeping basswood, weeping spruce, poplars, weeping hemlock, peach trees.

did.unittitle: Local landscapes, travels, transplanting, cutting and spraying experiments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Print numbers 185-280.
